questions.stem: This micrograph contrasts the two principal cells of acute inflammation, with a neutrophil at left and a tissue macrophage at right. Approximately when does the cell shown at right become the predominant cell at the site of injury?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Immediately at the moment of injury

explanation.summary: Neutrophils arrive first and peak around 24 hours; macrophages become the predominant cell type roughly 48-72 hours after injury or infection.
explanation.whyCorrect: Neutrophils arrive first and peak around 24 hours; macrophages become the predominant cell type roughly 48-72 hours after injury or infection.

questions.stem: This chest radiograph shows regions of increased opacity within the lung fields. What process do these opacities most likely represent?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Alveolar infiltrate consistent with bacterial pneumonia

explanation.summary: The opaque areas correspond to alveolar spaces filled with purulent exudate, a pattern consistent with bacterial pneumonia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The opaque areas correspond to alveolar spaces filled with purulent exudate, a pattern consistent with bacterial pneumonia.

questions.stem: This skin section shows the epidermis lifted away from the underlying dermis by a collection of pale, watery fluid. Which morphologic pattern of inflammation is depicted?

explanation.summary: A blister formed by accumulation of protein-poor, cell-poor (transudate-like) fluid separating the epidermis represents serous inflammation.
explanation.whyCorrect: A blister formed by accumulation of protein-poor, cell-poor (transudate-like) fluid separating the epidermis represents serous inflammation.

questions.stem: Panel A shows a shaggy, roughened surface coating this heart and panel B shows a pink meshwork overlying the pericardial surface. Which type of inflammatory exudate is shown?

explanation.summary: Increased vascular permeability allows fibrinogen to leak and deposit as fibrin on the pericardial surface, producing a fibrinous exudate (fibrinous pericarditis).
explanation.whyCorrect: Increased vascular permeability allows fibrinogen to leak and deposit as fibrin on the pericardial surface, producing a fibrinous exudate (fibrinous pericarditis).

questions.stem: This gross specimen (A) and low-power cross-section (B) show a punched-out defect penetrating through the mucosal surface with an inflammatory exudate at its base. Which morphologic pattern of inflammation is shown?

explanation.summary: An ulcer is a local defect of an epithelial surface produced by sloughing of inflamed necrotic tissue, here a chronic duodenal ulcer.
explanation.whyCorrect: An ulcer is a local defect of an epithelial surface produced by sloughing of inflamed necrotic tissue, here a chronic duodenal ulcer.
